preFix.title: Simple versus aerated compost tea: which to make and when
preFix.subtitle: Understanding the two approaches to liquid compost extract
preFix.excerpt: Compost tea starts with finished compost steeped in water. The simple (non-aerated) method produces a dilute liquid feed with soluble nutrients from the compost. The aerated method bubbles air through the liquid for 24 to 48 hours, multiplying the bacterial and fungal populations before they are diluted and applied. The two products are different in nature and application. Knowing which to make prevents wasted effort and misapplied results.
preFix.sourceNotes: Jeff Lowenfels and Wayne Lewis, Teaming with Microbes (2010), chapters on compost tea. RHS horticultural guidance on liquid feeds. USDA AMS National Organic Program guidance on compost tea safety 2004.

content.text: is made by placing a few handfuls of finished compost in a hessian bag or old pillowcase, dropping it into a bucket of water, and steeping for 24 to 48 hours. The liquid turns brown and contains humic acids, some minerals, and a small population of microorganisms from the compost. It is a dilute liquid feed, roughly equal to a weak seaweed or fish fertiliser application.

uses a small aquarium pump (5 to 20 litre/hour) to bubble air through the same brew. After 24 to 48 hours, aerobic bacteria and fungi have multiplied 100 to 1,000-fold under good conditions. The result is not mainly a nutrient feed. It is a microbial inoculant that puts living organisms back into soil that has been disturbed, sterilised, or depleted.
Simple compost tea, for a general liquid feed on established plants during the growing season. Dilute 1:5 or more before applying to leaves or soil. Apply within 24 hours of making.

ACT, for soil inoculation after digging, adding fresh raised-bed compost, or planting into tired soil. The bacteria and fungi colonise the rhizosphere and support root health. Apply directly to soil, not to plant foliage, because the microorganisms need to reach soil to be effective.
Fill a cloth bag (or old pillowcase) with about 1 litre of finished, well-rotted compost.
Lower the bag into 10 litres of water (tap water is fine; if you have a water butt, use that, no chlorine). Stir once.
Leave for 24 to 48 hours in a warm spot. Stir occasionally.
Dilute the tea 1:5 before watering onto soil or using as a foliar spray. Do not apply undiluted, the concentration of humic acids can suppress germination.
As above, but add a small amount of molasses (1 tablespoon per 10 litres) to the water as a food source for the bacteria.
Insert the aquarium pump airline at the base of the bucket. Run it continuously for 24 to 36 hours. The liquid should be actively bubbling, not just surface-agitated.
Apply within 4 hours of turning off the pump. Once aeration stops, the aerobic bacteria deplete the dissolved oxygen quickly and die. ACT is not stored; it is made and used the same day.

Do not apply compost tea to the edible parts of plants within 90 days of harvest if the source compost contained animal manures. For home compost made from kitchen waste and garden materials only, this restriction does not apply. Do not apply to salad crops at the foliar level, the mechanical splashing risk of any liquid onto leaves near harvest is the same for any liquid feed.
